I don't care a ton, but some may so I must note it again. Bass quality wore out over time. Its not terrible, but you can tell. Loses that "theater" feel to it, sounds like moderate quality headphones now which have decent bass but no "bass boost" if that makes sense. I have a set of headphones provided at work, and I can tell the difference, that high quality deep "bass" went away at some point on these. I didn't even notice until I tried a different set of headphones, so if this is your only set of headphones you won't even notice but if you care greatly about it, these headphones have it, but I don't know how long and it can wear out.

Reminder, this is from a set 5 years ago. Things can change a lot for a company in that time, quality may differ from back then but in 2015 these are in my mind unbeatable at this price.

reviews.author: Ariana Phouangkeo
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on March 7, 2021
reviews.content:
It sounds great for casual listeners, but it won't please Audiophiles (of course). It comes with a carry bag and a removable/attachable 6.5mm jack. Disclaimer: I am not Audio-literate, I only go off of what I know and hear. I have bought these for everyday use on my desktop as I do not need a headset with a microphone to sacrifice parts of the headphones.

I used the 6.5mm on a digital and analog receiver and it sounds great for my usage. Even though it won't get all of the frequencies or be of best quality, it is still good to a casual listener. It isn't a Sennheiser, but it definitely isn't Skullcandy. I have watched tonnes of videos on what Headphones to buy and this was on my radar because of the Studio-like qualities and comfort it had for the price. Don't get me wrong, I know these made for Studio standards, but It definitely can be used in one.

The ear cups are also very soft and comfy for hours of use without any headaches or strain. I wear glasses normally and it doesn't push the frame into my skull over time like other headphones that I have used.
The folding feature is also a big plus. Its very light and easy to carry around. I was surprised when I lifted them from the box as it was less than a pound. They are not comfortable to have sitting on your neck however, and I have found as it is a bit tight and small for it to be worn that way. Best thing to do otherwise is to just let one ear off the headphones.

The plastic and metal bits are the best quality for this price, and the product does not feel cheap in the slightest. And I'm almost confident that these ear cups won't flake off fake leather like material like my old Razer Krakens.

Is it worth the price? In my eyes yes, even with the fact that I haven't listened on anything of better quality. For everyday use I am really glad that these work really well and don't cause any uncomfortable, Who cares, as long as it fits my needs, it fits.

reviews.author: Matthew
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on February 28, 2026
reviews.content: I really like my Audio-Technica M30x headphones. The sound quality is great, they fit perfectly on my head, and they’re definitely an upgrade from the bundle set headphones I was using before. They help me mix better when engineering, and they also sound great for gaming too.

reviews.author: Dieselog
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on February 19, 2026
reviews.content: It's early to review this product but so far my only regret is not making the purchase sooner. Near complete privacy from the outside and a superior listening experience on the inside. These have me wondering how much better can a set of phones sound in this price range? It's also tempting to see how much better an expensive pair would sound. You know that audiophile trap. "Those Ohm Walsh 4's sound great, let's push the budget..." If there is anything missing in these phones, it's the mid-range.

reviews.author: J. Bowe
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on April 7, 2025
reviews.content:
There are three things you need to know about me: I have sensitive hearing, love bass, and I'm a huge Bose fan. These headphones are better than my Bose home speakers!

Got these puppies today, plugged them in, and queued up Charles Mingus' "Haitian Fight Song." (Which, imo, is the ultimate test of any audio equipment. If you can hear Mingus doing his thang in the background without the trumpet blasting your ears off, you've got good stuff!) These headphones performed admirably; I heard stuff (like string noise) that I had never heard before!

I will admit, however, that these are flat equalized, and maybe that's the difference between headphones and monitors? At any rate, MOAR BASS. So I loaded up some Type O Negative. Beautiful, beautiful, sublime and clear sound, but the bass was once again in the background, not front and center like I'm used to. So, if you're a bass nerd, you might want to EQ these to your liking.

Pros: Sounds like they should cost WAY more than they do, no distortion at high (>80%) volume, super lightweight, and comfortable on my kid-sized head.

Cons: Might need an equalizer if you're not happy with the flat EQ. Literally, that's my only con.

reviews.author: scribe
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on June 10, 2014
reviews.content:
The big brothers to these headphones, the M50x, are constantly tossed about on audiophile websites as a much better sounding/lower cost alternative to overhyped headphones like Beats. However, I did not want to spend the money on the M50x's as more of a casual user. However, after hearing the great sound out of these, I'd really like to give those a listen. However, this is a review of the 30's, so let's get to it.

Pros:
Sound. Really great sound. Crisp highs, clear mids, and lows that are strong without being overpowering. I've tried some pricier cans, and these hang right there with them.

Comfort. These were comfortable enough that I can wear them for an hour or two at a time with no issue.

Portability. While these are quite a bit bigger than your earbuds you tote around, these do fold up to make them easier to carry.

Price. Really hard to beat the price for the sound you get out of these. If the $70 is still a little pricey, I'd recommend checking out last years model of the ATH-M30.

Cons:
Input jack piece. This comes with the larger adapter that screws on. However, I found that since the jack has a larger base to allow the adapter to screw on, it didn't work well with my iPhone case. It worked find with my iPad and other applications, but I'd be cautious of size of what you are using.

Removable cord:
This does not have the removable cord that the 40x and 50x has, which would have been really nice. I knew this going into the purchase, so I didn't knock it down a star. Not a deal breaker for me...just a nicety.

Notes:
The cord itself, for me could have fit into both pros and cons. It is very long...like around 11 feet. This could be a pro or con for you depending on your application. Using them on a computer and moving around...the long cord is nice. Trying to loop it up to use them portably, show you that this isn't really what these are for.

Overall, I would definitely recommend these.

reviews.author: Johnny Linden
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on March 31, 2023
reviews.content: Don't pay much attention to these reviews for these fabulous Sennheiser RS 120 On-Ear Wireless Headphones with Crystal-Clear audio voice sound. You just have to learn the little tricks to know how to easily use them. #1. Most earphones that cost less than $100 don't have clear sound. The voices will be muffled and very hard to understand. These RS 120 earphones have crystal clear voice audio. #2. If you can't get loud enough volume on the earphones from your TV & or computer, you'll have to hook up a stereo amplifer between your TV or computer and the charging dock, like your usual am/fm cd/dvd amps and speakers used with desktop computers and speakers you use for great sound out of your computer. Plug your audio out from your TV into the cd or dvd in on the amp and the audio out of your computer into the other input. Then you can easily increase the volume going into your earphones and still use the volume control on the earphones. Be sure to buy an amplifer that has an earphone plug, preferably the larger 1/4 inch stereo earphones out plug and a 1/4" to 1/8" adapter that will hook up to the smaller 1/8" cords with 3 contacts. The 1/4" headphone plugs are much better quality than the 1/8" Plugs and will last much longer and if they short out, you can buy a new adapter. You can find a really good small 8"x10" amplifer on Amazon here for $56 that also supports blue tooth. https://www.amazon.com/Moukey-Bluetooth-Power-Amplifier-System/dp/B08H4R221Z/ref=sr_1_7?crid=113XWE3UC8KS0&keywords=stereo+amplifier&qid=1680275974&sprefix=stereo+%2Caps%2C120&sr=8-7 I have one of these and they have great reviews. Be sure to get one that has both bass & treble controls if you want clear voice audio. #3. If the audio stops on the earphones because a song stops playing or video stops, while you are reading an article on another link on your computer, the headphones will beep every 5 minutes to remind you no audio is playing so you can restart some audio sound or flip the switch off on the headphones to conserve battery life . Most headphones are leather that causes you ears to sweat. Here is a link for some $15 cloth velour replacement earpads on Amazon. https://www.amazon.com/RS120-RS119-Ear-Pads-Replacement/dp/B0913BNQTB/ref=sr_1_34?crid=1Q712Y96OU9HR&keywords=replacement+earpads+for+sennheiser+rs120+velour+earphones&qid=1683501883&sprefix=replacement+earpads+for+sennheiser+rs120+velour+earphones%2Caps%2C122&sr=8-34 velour replacement earpads for Sennheiser RF 120 earphones $15 #4. These Sennheiser rf or radio frequency headphones, not bluetooth, are very easy to match to your tv & computer just by turning them on. They also have a rotating volume control that works much easier than the than the push button volume controls on most headphones. #5. These on-ear RF headphones are light as a feather at only 8 oz are the most comfortable headphones you will ever use. You can barely feel them on your ears and wear them for hours without your ears hurting. They fit so loose, you have to be careful to keep them balanced on your head so they won't fall off when you tip your head forward or back, especially over the toilet. You can solve this problem by ordering replacement soft velour earpads for $15 that are wider and will hold the earphones from moving around. Both sets of earphones are not vinal or leather that sweats on your ears. They are made of soft velour that doesn't sweat. Be sure to get earphones covered with velour. #6. It is easy to get them to charge. Just drop the earphones squarely on the chrome magnetic rails one time, and don't touch them for a few seconds and the charging light will come on and start charging, unless they are fully charged. #7. They also have 3 different settings for audio quality at the bottom of the charging dock. Far left is best for clear voice audio and added bass. Center position, the audio is muffled. Far right has sharp clear audio with less bass. With these simple tricks, you are going to love your new R F headphones. 8. These Sennheiser RF 120 headphones have a range of 200 feet unless they have to go through brick walls or steel doors. If you usually use them on your computer & TV, in the same room, you will have plenty of signal range. Once you try them, you will love them even though they have one sad drawback that you can also solve with some black Gorilla duct tape. You will drop them sooner or later and they are very fragel and an earphone will break off and render them useless. You can fix this before they break by pushing them all the way together, where they aren't adjustable any more because you need to wrap black Gorilla duct tape across the crack where they slide in & out. Wrap the tape around twice right above the top of the headphone & over the crack where they adjust. You might as well tape them the minute they arrive, before you drop them. This should solve the problem of how fragel they are and keep them from breaking. The are the most comfortable headphones with the most crystal clear sound and great bass you will ever buy for less than $500. They're awesome for their price. You just have to be careful with them.

reviews.author: keith
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on January 7, 2026
reviews.content: The hook up to your tv are rca or 3.5 jack. You also can add a analog/digital powered adapter to infra red out on your tv. Have a approx 10 year ago model of this wireless headphone. It is old now, the rca cord comes right out of the base and all the insulation on the wire is cracked and missing. It no longer is stero. Also the ear piece where the batteries are have built up a tar substance which is sticky. Still works, still reliable. They should show this on their display instead of having others show this. The pair I just purchased sound is 50 times better than what we have. Have ordered another set. Light weight, easy to set up, no lag on sound with these during video/audio play like bluetooth, which is why we again bought these. More expensive than last year because of Donny's slush fund. Check your tv out audio jacks to see if these will work. There is always a way to connect to your tv with adapters. Love these headphones.

reviews.author: BJ Ried
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on July 22, 2023
reviews.content: Due to hearing loss and an older, non-bluetooth TV I bought a pair of these 4 or 5 years ago and they were awesome. Great sound and never lost a signal. I have no idea what Sennheiser did to this newer model but it is just horrible. I retuned my first pair due to the fact the sound just cut out constantly. Sometimes after an hour or so, sometimes every 5 or 10 minutes. I thought maybe the rechargeble batteries were old or bad, but I didn't want to have to buy new batteries for a new headset so I returned and got a new set. They were OK for a while then the same thing started. Dropped sound. Turn headphones off. Turn back on, listen to the annoying introduction sound, then back to whatever I was watching. Lather. Rinse. Repeat. So I broke down and bought some new batteries. It was OK for a while but then starting dropping again. And again. And again. I cannot stress how annoying this is, to be watching a program and just lose the sound, then have to restart the whole process. Unfortunately my window has closed to return these for good, so I am stuck. Or I'll just just stop using them and admit defeat. These headphones were one of my worst purchases on Amazon, where I usually have good luck in finding quality products. I hope anyone who purchases these has better luck and a better experience than I have had. But I wholeheartedly no not recommend these headphones. Sorry Sennheiser.

reviews.author: Julie
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on February 26, 2026
reviews.content: Have an old pair of Sennheiser headphones which are great, but this (RS 120-W) was cutting out periodically.

reviews.author: Fred Raimer
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on August 3, 2024

reviews.author: Little Voice
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on January 27, 2026
reviews.content: The sound is the most important- and these are created for the best audio equipment delivering the sound, so also makes all other provide the best they are able. They are also, super light and all other engineering of both the headset and the charging/signal sending unit are standout superb. Even when I wear them upstairs and outside with door shut! We wear them typically three hours -/day and our first set was awesome for 15 years, then purchased new Sennheisers!

reviews.author: Steve
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on December 6, 2023
reviews.content:
I bought the Sennheiser RS-120W headphones to replace a set of Sennhieser RS-120 headphones that were stolen. The two sets are essentially identical and have good sound quality, but the old set [without the "W" in the model number] had a tuning dial that could be used to increase the range. This control allowed me a range of approximately 90-100 feet. The replacement set, RS-120W, has no such tuning control and gives me a range of around 60 feet max--generally more like 40-50 feet. In some cases, this is with the signal passing through two or three walls, but I still think this range is disappointing, and I have a hard time believing most people achieve the 185 foot range claimed for this model by Sennheiser. Again, the sound quality is good, but Bluetooth headphones, in my experience, give comparable or better range, typically cost less, sound just as good to my ears, and don't require a base station. I would not buy these again.
UPDATE December 28 2023: These headphones sometimes lose connection completely while well within expected range--sometimes as little as 15 feet away from the base unit. The headphones must then be turned off and turned back on. They always relocate the signal, but this is an another strike against these headphones which are, in my opinion, way over-priced and inferior to Bluetooth headphones.

reviews.author: Lottie Kearns
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on January 30, 2025
reviews.content: We hook this Sennheiser RS 120 W to our TV because my husband has a hearing problem. We live in a condo and cannot blast the TV. He can turn the sound off of the TV and use the headset that comes with this Sennheiser RS 120 W and turn up the volume on the headset as much as he needs it. This Sennheiser is amazing! Well, we've had it for a month now and thought it was amazing, but now .... it won't charge! None of the lights come up. It doesn't turn on and it doesn't charge. I thought it was great for the first month, but now, I am not happy with it at all. I contacted Sennheiser, but they won't do anything to fix the problem. I am very disappointed now.

reviews.author: Jeffrey L.
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on October 7, 2024
reviews.content:
BOTTOM LINE: Call clarity!! These are - by a large margin - the best-performing set of wireless headphones that we've ever had in the household. And we've tried at least a DOZEN pairs over the past few years, maybe more. My wife has these now, and I'll be getting a pair shortly.

For us, it all comes down to call clarity, where these simply reign supreme. Any pair of headphones is useless for making calls unless the recipient can hear you clearly, right? These headphones WORK for that purpose!

When the wife is in the car and gives me a call, I can hear her as clear as day, and arguably better than when she's using the phone itself. When there's background noise (noises at work, road noise, etc.), these miraculously filter it out. When she first had them on, I was taken aback as to why she sounded so sharp on the other end. I figured she was in a quiet room somewhere, but no...she was on the highway...in Boston...in traffic...yet her voice was sharp, loud, and consistent. And THAT'S why I'm calling these the best pair we've ever had.

Additionally, everything else is fantastic. The battery life is great, the functions work just as advertised, and - for a wife who works in liquor distribution - they also look pretty sweet (people pick up on the contrast between her fancy work outfits and the ubiquitous bright yellow of the DeWalt brand).

Altogether, these are an absolute winner if you want a tough, functional, highly water-resistant, comfortable set of wireless headphones. In my line of work, I expect DeWalt to put out a quality tool, whatever it may be...and these definitely maintain the bar set by my yellow brand. Nicely done!

reviews.author: Ernie
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on January 21, 2026
reviews.content: Very sturdy and good sound. Compatible with my phone, tv, and tablet. The color makes them noticeable and less prone to accidents. I feel they work ideally as designed.

reviews.author: KD
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on February 13, 2026
reviews.content: Heavy duty . I work construction and use them all day every day. They last a couple of years before the wires break. Great for taking phone calls and continuing to work. They also make pretty good ear plug. And you won't lose one because they are around your neck.

reviews.author: Oscar
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on December 3, 2025
reviews.content: The Dewalt headphones impress with a sturdy, durable design that feels built to withstand daily use. Their long-lasting battery life provides the freedom to move comfortably around the house, staying connected without the hassle of frequent recharging. Thoughtful engineering also delivers crystal-clear phone calls, ensuring conversations sound sharp and professional. While the audio quality for music is solid but fairly standard, these headphones excel as a reliable tool for work and everyday communication, combining durability, convenience, and clarity in one package.

reviews.author: David Daniels
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on December 27, 2025
reviews.content: The clearest neckband headphones I've ever had. Not bassy, but decent sound. The big payout is the clarity of calls and suppression of background noise. People I talk to on the phone notice the clarity. The magnetic clasping means I know where the hanging ear buds are. Way worth the modest price. Last at least 2 days on a charge. Perfect size for under the collar. Connect perfectly with bluetooth. Sturdy ear bud cables.

reviews.author: Amazon Customer
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on February 15, 2026
reviews.content: Better than the previous version, which lasted for years of outside work. Never had a problem with rain or sweat. I like the magnetic buds. Also bluetooth range is much better on this version. Works well for my needs. Sound quality isn’t top notch but I don’t need it. Very happy with these

reviews.author: Supergirl
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on March 6, 2026
reviews.content: The battery has not died on them yet. The battery life is really excellent. They are comfortable to wear. The sound 85% of them time is excellent for microphone. The sound for listening is clear 100% of the time. I recommend claiming a warranty on these with your purchase. I like that it’s one piece not two. Excellent business tool, excellent recreational toy. Pair with the case .

reviews.author: Lisa Bev
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on January 30, 2026
reviews.content: Its okay. The sound quality is decent but the setup is annoying. Instead of having the ear pieces tuck into the headset or magnetize to it, they magnetize to each other. So if you're wearing just one ear piece so you can hear on walks the other just dangles and gets in the way. It also doesnt autostart the audio from my phone even though it's connected.

reviews.author: Eric McLeish
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on January 18, 2026
reviews.format: Color: Black CopperStyle: ACCENTUM Special Edition
reviews.content:
For reference, I got the limited edition black/copper Accentums.

Came as advertised in a very nice box (which I'm keeping). Nothing was over the top, and the instructions are to the point & digestible.

The audio quality is something of dreams to say the least; not to mention at my lowest volume, I can listen to all my songs comfortably with no details in the audio lost.

The only downside is the ANC or Transparency modes always being on with the only options being to toggle between the two.
Switching between either mode is seamless, and they work very well for the price range, but the inability to toggle them off completely is what seems to tank the already capable battery.

- great sound quality
- decent battery (just turn them off when not in use if you don't wanna charge them every 3 days)
- super portable (neck wearing is a little iffy tho)
- nice colors, I like them; that's why I got this one
- very comfortable fit

They're now my daily drivers 😌👍

reviews.author: ML Kindle Customer
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on November 17, 2025
reviews.content: Anybody that owns these probably knows what I'm getting ready to say. I was looking for headphones and I was already aware that there is a difference between low quality middle quality and high quality. I had always heard the name Sennheiser and how they were a higher quality headphone but the price always seem out of reach. I bought these because I want to replace an old pair that I had that I had worn out in the pad had begun to show. So far I can say the padding that they have is excellent it is a soft leather with a deep beige color. I receive good sound good reception. I love the size and they connect really easy. The only downside I would say is when I purchase these they did not come with a carrying Sack or carrying case. I feel like something of this value I should be able to put up in something to maintain it. Functionality wise these are very ergonomic the design allows you to move the headphone 180 degrees while the earphone itself slides relatively easy up inside of the arch itself. Their shows that there was some intellect and Engineering regarding these. The only other thing that I can say would be a knock on these is if you don't wear them for a while they automatically cut off and you don't know when that will be. The other thing is that when I cut them on it seems to take quite a second but it's well worth it which is why I'm still giving this five stars. The sound quality and base that I get Rivals it along with other high quality headphones such as Bose

reviews.author: Z. zhou
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on September 8, 2025
reviews.format: Color: Black CopperStyle: ACCENTUM Special Edition
reviews.content:
The sound quality is impressive – vocals are clear and natural, bass is powerful but never overwhelming, and the wide soundstage brings music to life with depth and detail.

Compared with the Bose QC35 II, there are both strengths and weaknesses. Comfort is the biggest drawback: the clamping force is tight, and extended wear causes pressure and even pain on the head. Usability is also less intuitive, making everyday operation less convenient. Noise isolation is just average – it reduces some outside sounds but doesn’t match the passive comfort or active noise canceling quality of the QC35 II.

On the other hand, phone call performance is excellent. Voices come through smooth and clear, with background noise handled far better than the QC35 II, making it a strong choice if you take calls often.

In short, it delivers better sound quality and far superior call clarity, but falls behind the QC35 II in comfort, usability, and noise isolation.

reviews.author: JMB
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on January 8, 2026
reviews.content: These are a good, mid-tier, Bluetooth, ANC headphone. In this category, they are Sennheiser's mid-hundred dollar offering. I use mine on airplanes and occasionally while I am working around the house so I can't say if they are audiophile quality, the sound is good for me and better than earbuds in my opinion. I like the fact that they surround the ear rather than pressing on it, I find that better for the ANC and long term wear. (I have had head phones with smaller ear cups that pressed on my ears painfully.) Being mid-tier they don't come with a case. I found a Ginsco case on here that is about 2" deep and works really well.

reviews.author: Camila Ismail
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on January 21, 2026
reviews.content: After using these headphones almost everyday since November of 2025 I have fallen in love. The sound quality is lovely and the free Sennheiser plus app offers many different settings for listeners to customize their listening experience. I also loved the soft cream color that I ended up getting. The only complaint I have is that the volume cannot be adjusted to be lower on a phone or laptop. The lowest volume it can be used at is as low as a phone’s 30% volume before disconnecting any volume. This is just a preference for me though. The sound transparency vs the sound canceling is also very cool. The sound canceling setting sounds like rain droplets all dropping in the background. The top head cushion is made from a soft almost suede feeling rubber layer that has added harder cushioning inside this thin rubber layer. Also if you like a softer cushioning at the top then this may not be for you because it is harder. The ear cushions though create a proper all around ear seal that fit comfortably arround your ear. And the side adjustments move really smoothly to adjust to your head shape as you put them on. Lastly the ears move to remain flat or to fit on the sides of your head. I tell you these are really good! I bought a second in black.

reviews.author: Amazon Customer
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on August 25, 2025
reviews.format: Color: Black CopperStyle: ACCENTUM Special Edition
reviews.content:
Buyer beware!

While the product as a whole is fantastic it only lasts for 6 months and then proceeds to mechanically break down. Mine is already having violently loud tapping noises on the left ear which hurts like hell after a while. It's unbearable. So, if you don't mind buying headphones every 6 months for the cheapest built headphone on the planet with quality sound but cost $144 bucks buy this one. If you want something that last longer than 6 months buy elsewhere.

I've had lots of headphones from different companies some lasted a year some lasted longer. Shockingly it's always the cheap ones that last the longest. Weird stuff. Good news is it does last a long time on the charge, so you get to enjoy it a LONG time like 2 days' worth of listening for those 6 months. Clarity is great for ASMR. Volume control is ehh, nothing special here. Doesn't weigh too hard on the head either. Just once more as a I said. Physical mechanical quality so bad tearing it open won't save it. Trust me I tried. It stopped the issue for a few minutes and then it came back. Maybe if I rearrange the wires differently or try to pull them away from each other it might work, but dude. The nails are already a pain to unscrew out. You gotta pull the earmuffs off. Then shove a screwdriver down into these squishy parts hiding the screws and remove those. Then unscrew these tiny screws then once you get those off the wires are TIGHTLY crammed together layered on top of each other. Like no wonder there is wiring issues, it's REALLY tightly crammed together. It was bound to experience signal bleed off eventually. Yes, every headphone set I've bought from Amazon when they fail, I tear them apart. Shockingly this one didn't have a power button failure, but then again, they built their power button to not fail as hard as some brands with the whole reach around on/off deal. Those are SO HARD TO FIX. So at least you know your headset won't fail from a faulty power button.

reviews.author: Ian T.
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on December 23, 2025
reviews.content: Bought these after my Skullcandy Crushers broke, and they definitely are very nice, but there's some disappointing aspects. They are very comfortable for long term wear, and the audio quality is amazing. Some caveats I've experienced are the battery life isn't on par with other headphones brands, and the bass output is pretty much nonexistent for bass-heavy fans, and there isn't any way to use the Accentum software on a PC or laptop, which is disappointing. The headphones are comfortable, have great noise cancellation, and sounds great, but for the price tag, I would encourage others to look into other options for the price.

reviews.author: J + S forever
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on January 15, 2026
reviews.format: Color: Black CopperStyle: ACCENTUM Special Edition
reviews.content: I used to have a full vintage stereo system that I loved. For the past 15 years, I've been living with portable Bluetooth speakers. Buying these headphones has reminded me how much I love music. For the price, they are excellent. I've had no functionality issues. They're comfortable. They seem to be durable. The sound quality, again, for this price is excellent. When I look to upgrade, I will definitely be looking at Sennheiser again.

reviews.author: GringoGaige
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on January 19, 2026
reviews.content:
The Beats Solo 4 – Wireless On-Ear Bluetooth Headphones deliver exactly what I was hoping for. The sound is clear, punchy, and well-balanced, with strong bass that doesn’t overpower vocals or mids. They’re great for music, podcasts, and calls.

They’re lightweight and comfortable to wear for long periods, and the on-ear design feels secure without being too tight. Battery life is excellent—I can go days without needing to recharge—and pairing with devices is fast and seamless.

Overall, these headphones are stylish, dependable, and high quality, just what you’d expect from Beats. Highly recommended if you want great wireless sound with solid everyday comfort.

reviews.author: Asia
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on February 4, 2026
reviews.content: These headphones are my new favorite thing ever. Got them as a gift to myself. So worth it. Battery life is incredible. Only need to charge fully like once a week, and I go to the gym daily. Incredible sound quality - but I expect nothing less from Beats. Color is super vibrant and absolutely cute. Can't hear a thing with them on except my music, unless its playing low, then I can hear other people talking - which is good for when I'm at the gym with others, I dont have to pull it off my ear to hear them. Can wear them for a few hours, but I can say they make my ears sore after that. But I'm not pressed about that really.

reviews.author: Sunny
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on March 4, 2026
reviews.content: Love the sound, the quality. I usually don’t like headphones because they’re always too tight on my head, but these are very comfortable. Got them on sale, good price!

reviews.author: Romeo Six Actual
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on February 3, 2026
reviews.content:
I absolutely love the Beats Solo 4 Wireless On-Ear Headphones! The sound quality is amazing, and the battery life is incredible—up to 50 hours means I rarely have to charge them. They’re super lightweight and comfortable for long listening sessions.

The pink color is awesome and really stands out. Stylish, functional, and everything I hoped for. Highly recommend!

reviews.author: Ellis Andral
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on February 7, 2026
reviews.content: This is a 4.8/5 for me. Solid feel on the head, slightly more snug than I’d prefer but I’m going to assume that it will slowly get better with usage (no points off there). The only qualm I have is the audio cable that it comes with (this is replacing the cable-only Beats headphones that appears to have stopped being produced, I’m an MP3 player person when it comes to music). The audio cable suppresses the bass by a noticeable amount if you care about sound quality. Wish I didn’t have to get a 3rd party cable to get around this, but it feels good knowing that the headphones themselves produce awesome sound quality.

reviews.author: Stephanie
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on November 24, 2025
reviews.content:
I see the black Friday deal is the same amount I paid in March. It’s very deceptive when companies do this, and label it as a “Black Friday deal” making you think you’re getting a good price.
Anyway. I like these a lot. The battery life is super long and the sound is really good. They do a good job at muting outside noise and they fold up quite small. The biggest issue I have is comfort. They are not the most comfortable headphones. After sitting on your ears for 20 or so minutes, I find myself adjusting them. After 30-40 minutes my ears start to hurt. I can see discomfort after an extended amount of time, but I wouldn’t expect to feel discomfort after less than an hour. I know they have an option that fits over the ear, and I would probably opt for that one if I’m ever in the market again.
I don’t know if they are worth the price. I haven’t tried other brands, but they were very easy to set up on my phone, and they connect right away when turned on. Overall I really
like these a lot, aside from the discomfort and misleading “sale” price.

reviews.author: Lumberjack Bear
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on February 14, 2026
reviews.content: These headphones are very comfortable ,sound excellent , built to last , plus Bluetooth connectivity is totally effortless with my IPad. They charge up super fast. I particularly like the option to connect via a headphone jack instead of only using wireless. The cord is included in addition to the charging cable all tucked away in the headphone case. The headphones 'fold' up to fit in the case securely. Wonderful design

reviews.author: William Meeker
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on February 23, 2026
reviews.content:
I grabbed the Beats Solo 4 for my daughter for Christmas and these absolutely stole the show. The look, the sound, the whole package just hit. As soon as she put them on, that was it. Instant favorite gift.

They’re lightweight and comfortable, and the battery life is no joke. Not having to constantly charge them is a big win. The sound quality is strong and clear, with solid bass without being muddy.

Only downside? She wears them in the car now and completely zones out during our car karaoke sessions. I guess I created my own competition.

If you’re looking for a gift that will seriously impress, these did the job. Christmas victory secured.

reviews.author: Phoenix B.
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on February 3, 2026
reviews.format: Color: Deep BrownStyle: Beats Studio Pro
reviews.content:
I am reviewing this item in brand new condition. If you get a refurbished somewhere else, the shipping will probably differ a lot too.

1. Shipping - Shipped in hard shell, extra sturdy case inside a second zipped plush case. The plush case that comes with this headset is very, very nice and you will likely not replace it due to quality.

2. Sound - I wanted to give this headset several hours a day every day testing before reviewing. I've used them for calls, gaming, and jamming out via connected bluetooth to my PC and Phone. The best description that can be given for the sound quality is instead of having to use a custom 'sound profile' to get 'the best' sound, this headset gives you the sound exactly as it was 'written', directly to you. If you do any audio engineering you're familiar with panning, or simulated directions in video games. This one is really good. For music listening it's loud enough I don't need to play it at max volume, it doesn't cause listening fatigue. I've never enjoyed three hours of music without even realizing it until this set.

3. Comfort/texture. I rubbed my fingertips on the cup several times because it's tingly. My head is really small and all headphones are too big for me, but children's are too small, and this is actually still holding snug to my ears so the noise cancelling works like a charm. I've had other headsets at the 150 price point loosen by week 2 and not fit so snug, so the noise cancelling wasn't "wow" anymore but this is the real deal. And unlike the other headsets, turning on noise cancelling doesn't hum or blast static into your ears, or make the sound a little murky. I've worn these for several hours daily and they have a breathableness to them, somehow. It doesn't make my jaw ache. The tension (cup to ear) hasn't loosened like other models have.

4.) Mic - If you accidentally connect it to your PC instead of your actual dedicated microphone it affects audio quality severely because it goes into some 'hybrid' mode where you're meant to hear yourself talk but it's terrible for extraction shooter or any 'my environment gives me important ques' kind of games. I did NOT buy this headset for talking into it, but spent a very terrible round of Arc Raiders not being able to hear the map lol. Just noting. I use the headset to play audio, my studio condenser mic to talk into and in that 'setup' the audio is fantastic and so is my mic!

5) Charge/reliability/battery life: I plug them in when I got to the kitchen and before bed, they've never died. Yeah, I game and jam, but I'm also a game developer who takes calls and streams unexpectedly throughout the day as a fully remote worker. If the 4 white light icons represent charge then they've never fallen below 3/4 on this schedule. You can also used them plugged in from headset to desktop directly if that 'crisis' were to befall you. The bluetooth range is also farther than I expected, I can walk way into the kitchen past 3 walls and they stay connected with clear audio.

So yeah, assuming they can hold up for 1 year of daily use like this I'd buy them again to refresh them the next year.

reviews.author: AddyTalk
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on January 31, 2026
reviews.content:
The Beats Studio headphones deliver solid sound quality with strong bass, clear mids, and effective active noise cancellation. They’re comfortable for long listening sessions, pair easily with Apple devices, and battery life has been reliable for daily use. Build quality feels premium, and they work well for travel, workouts, and casual listening.

That said, I would not recommend paying full price. These headphones go on sale very frequently—often dropping to $179.99—and at that price, they’re an excellent value. At MSRP, there are stronger competitors in the same range.

If you can wait for a sale (which usually doesn’t take long), they’re absolutely worth buying. Just be patient and don’t overpay.

reviews.author: Ryan Brown
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on January 12, 2026
reviews.content: These headphones stand out because they get everything right where it matters most: size, comfort, sound, and ease of use. The over-ear design fully covers even larger ears without pressing on them, making them comfortable for long listening sessions while still feeling lightweight and secure. The sound quality is clear and balanced with deep but controlled bass, and features like noise canceling and transparency make it easy to either block out the world or stay aware when needed. Physical control buttons make adjusting volume or skipping tracks quick and intuitive, and switching between Apple devices is smooth and effortless. They fold up neatly for portability, and when kept in a hard case, they’re easy to take anywhere without worry, making them an excellent everyday headphone. Battery life is long enough that I rarely think about charging them, and a quick top-up gives hours of use. Overall, they feel premium, reliable, and worth every dollar.

reviews.author: Schoolboy
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on January 21, 2026
reviews.format: Color: Sand GrayStyle: Beats Studio Pro
reviews.content: My wife asked me to update her work environment since they went to an open concept design at work. She needed something to meet with her staff remotely, with good noise cancelling, that was comfortaable to wear for extended periods and this product checked those boxes. It was easy to connect to her laptop for work and her phone for music. Bought it during a Prime Day so the cost was very reasonable. The sound has been better than my wife expected. She hasn't used any of the buttons, other than to turn it on, as she is controlling volume with the device. She like the non-black color (the only thing she didn't want was a black headset). I would buy again.

reviews.author: Alexa Burn
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on January 6, 2026
reviews.content:
⭐️⭐️⭐️⭐️ Love These Headphones! Just a Few Small Things
These are my first pair of over-ear headphones, and wow, I’m impressed! The sound quality is fantastic, and the noise cancelling is honestly amazing. I wear glasses, so after about two hours they start to feel a little uncomfortable, but that’s pretty normal for over-ear headphones.
Transparency mode is good, definitely better than the Beats Pro earbuds, but it’s not perfect. There’s a slight static sound when it’s on. If transparency is super important to you, I’d probably recommend checking out Apple AirPods (though I haven’t tried the AirPods Max yet).
I’ve always had trouble with earbuds falling out and eventually breaking after being dropped too many times, so I love how solid and durable these feel. Plus, the sandstone color is absolutely adorable! They’ve totally converted me to an over-ear headphone fan. If transparency is your top priority, you might want to keep lookingbut for sound quality and noise cancelling, these are awesome.

reviews.author: Diana Muriel
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on March 6, 2026
reviews.content: I bought the Beats Studio Pro headphones, and I absolutely love them! The sound quality is amazing, very clear with deep bass. They’re also really comfortable to wear for long periods. The design feels premium, and they’re very durable. Definitely worth the investment if you want high-quality headphones.

reviews.author: Breana Mcclellan
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on February 27, 2026
reviews.content:
I absolutely love my Beats Studio Pro headphones. The quality is amazing, and they’ve held up so well over the past year. I specifically wanted something that wasn’t super bulky, and these are the perfect size — sleek without looking oversized.

The color is also super cute, and I get compliments on them all the time! The sound quality and battery life have both been great, and I have zero regrets about spending the money on them. Definitely worth it.

reviews.author: christopher w. rushmore
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on February 14, 2026
reviews.content: Absolutely love these! Noise canceling works almost too well. Sound is crisp, clear and full. Lightweight for the size, durable, and easy to pair. Easy to control with buttons on the left ear.

reviews.author: Stanton Nichols
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on January 25, 2026
reviews.content:
TL;DR?
The Sennheiser HD 560 S are extremely comfortable, lightweight, and easy to wear all day, with a secure fit that settles in nicely over time. Sonically, they offer outstanding value around $200 with an exceptionally neutral, well-balanced sound, wide soundstage, and strong resolution—making them excellent for critical listening, mixing, and mastering.
---
These headphones are simply fantastic! For me, maybe the easiest headphones I have ever worn "all day."

The clamp pressure is a bit tight out of the box, but not TOO tight. They WILL loosen up over time, so if you're sensitive to clamp, you might find yourself needing to "break them in."

On some over-ear headphones, I can struggle in finding that "this is where they sit on my head" position. Beyerdynamic Headphones can sound great, but it can be annoying fiddling with how they sit on your head until you find the "just right" position. The HD560s? Easy. Put them on, adjust, and you're good to go!

The materials here are what you expect! The earpads and headband are soft enough to comfortable, but firm enough to provide support. If you use them CONSTANTLY, you will eventually need to replace the earpads (1-2 years). The plastic is high quality, and there are nearly no metal parts. With that in mind: you could break these if you tried, but the plastic shouldn't fall apart any time soon. This makes them VERY lightweight, which is fantastic for comfort!

SOUND: the single most important aspect of this product. These sounds good. Really Really good. I would argue that this is potentially the best sound you can get around $200. These are extraordinarily well-balanced. What does that mean? It means they are very very neutral. They are not hyped, they are not "exciting." They are perfect for checking a mix and more critical listening. These have enough resolution to pick out fine adjustments in sound (like slight tube saturation). The soundstage is wide without being "too much," allowing for a solid depiction of the "Stereo Image" without adding additional confusion. Overall? They sound incredible! Very Neutral; not too "warm," just the right amount of balance for critical listening.

Overall? These are a fantastic choice for mixing and mastering. You can certainly use them for every day tasks as well, but some of the "punchy" or "crunchy" music might feel a little "anemic" under the microscope of the HD 560 S headphones. These are a true neutral. If you want to check balance? Pick these!

The only scenario I have ever found myself "reaching for something else" is grabbing my Beyerdynamic DT 900 Pro X headphones for better resolution/ detail in the high frequency. The 900 Pro X are not as neutral (or wide) as the HD 560 S, but their additional top-end extension makes them solid for "sanity checking" high frequency information. For tracking and monitoring, I would reach for something else (ATH-M40x or DT770 (maybe the DT 900 Pro X, they are basically semi-open back)).

reviews.author: wjones14
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on September 21, 2023
reviews.content:
I'm not an audiophile or headphone expert. I played drums as a teenager and later took guitar lessons for 10 years, still play the guitar almost daily, and love music in general. Hard rock has always been my favorite genre. I use headphones with my PC while watching YouTube music videos and concerts and also while playing video games like Cyberpunk 2077, Skyrim, Diablo IV, etc. I also have a pretty big library of old and new music on a 7th gen iPod Touch (now discontinued) and do some critical listening with that. I had been using budget headphones like the Koss KTXPro1 and Logitech G333 gaming earbuds, but always wanted to try something considered as "audiophile" headphones.

My son is somewhat of a headphone expert, so I reached out to him for buying guidance. His ideal headphones would be completely neutral, with no emphasis on either highs or lows. To start, he gave me a FiiO K3, which is a DAC/headphone amplifier. I didn't know I needed one, but he insisted. He also convinced me to download and install the open-source Equalizer APO application and its companion Peace UI component. It's a bit daunting for a newbie to get started with the headphone amp and equalizer software, but there are plenty of YouTube tutorial walkthroughs available.

In a few hours I had the headphone amp and the Peace interface working, and had separate configurations saved for the Koss and Logitech headphones. I downloaded pre-configured settings for each pair of headphones, which are set with the preferred "Harman Curve". I had no idea about any of this stuff previously. By the end of that day, I had learned how to tweak and save additional configurations, and now could choose between multiple pre-sets for each pair of headphones. For example, I created a pre-set that boosted the bass, another that boosted the highs, and one that boosted both bass and highs while leaving the middle frequencies at the standard Harman curve settings. That allowed me to experiment and decide my own personal preferences for music listening.

Just a primer - the headphone amp connects to the PC by USB, and it disables the built-in PC audio chip with a more powerful and clean signal. Then you plug your headphones into the headphone amp instead of the PC headphone input jack. Windows audio and volume is bypassed, so now the volume is controlled by the volume knob on the headphone amp. (You can turn off the headphone amp to listen through PC speakers or with the standard PC headphone jack.)

With the headphone amp and equalizer software, both my Koss and Logitech headphones sounded better than ever and I was hearing details that I wasn't hearing before. I had heard of headphone amps before, but never thought they were necessary. I couldn't imagine not using one now.

Then my son lent me his AKG K702 open back headphones to try. He likes them because they are pretty neutral. As he puts it, "you hear the music as the producer and engineer intended it to sound." The AKGs were the best headphones I had ever tried, hands down. I downloaded the pre-configured parametric EQ file for them and listened for a few days. I learned that I preferred a more bass-heavy sound, especially with the hard rock I usually listened to. (I am currently obsessed with a band named The Warning, but like many other artists including Spiritbox, Breaking Benjamin, Band-Maid, Evanescence, Muse, and older classic rock like Deep Purple, Pink Floyd, Zeppelin, Hendrix, and Van Halen.) Given that I liked a bit more bass emphasis, my son suggested the Sennheiser HD 560S.

I love them. With the standard EQ configuration, the low bass is a little lacking for my taste. I want the bass drum emphasized. So I created a pre-set with the bass boosted at 52 Hz and left the rest of frequencies alone. Perfect! I created another pre-set with the same 52 Hz boost and an additional boost at 5K Hz. I have some high frequency hearing loss, so this compensates for that, and allows me to hear the cymbals a little better. Normally I listen with the bass boost pre-set only and can hear everything fine.

The only minor gripe I have with the Sennheiser HD 5600S phones is the comfort level. The ear pads are soft and comfortable, and the clamping force isn't too much. But the headphones are somewhat bulky, especially compared to the Koss which are feather light and have almost zero clamping force. The Logitech earbuds are another story since they weigh virtually nothing and there's no clamping force. That said, I have used the Sennheisers for hours at a time and they're fine. You do forget they're on once you're immersed in the music or the game. I'll definitely choose the Sennheisers every time for critical listening. Occasionally I will use the Logitechs or Koss when watching non-music videos like interviews or product reviews, for the improved comfort level.

Here's a good website for scientifically tested objective headphone ratings: https://www.rtings.com/headphones

Definitely recommend the HD 560S as an entry level audiophile headphone. They're a tier above sub-$100 headphones for sure.

reviews.author: Lucas L.
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on January 16, 2026
reviews.content:
🎮 Gaming Audio & Positional Awareness
These open-back headphones provide a wide soundstage and excellent imaging, which helps you accurately pinpoint footsteps, gunfire, and directional cues — a huge advantage in FPS games and competitive play. The neutral sound signature means you’re not getting exaggerated bass booms or bloated effects that can mask subtle movements, so distant enemies and spatial cues feel easier to locate than with typical bass-heavy gaming headsets.

🧠 Advantage in Competitive Play
For titles where spatial awareness matters (like tactical shooters), the clarity and separation of sounds help you determine which direction an opponent is coming from and at what distance — making your audio cues more actionable than with many stock gaming headsets.

🎧 Comfort & Build for Long Sessions
The headphones are lightweight and comfortable enough for extended sessions, so marathon gaming nights don’t feel fatiguing. However, keep in mind they don’t include a microphone, so you’ll need an external mic if you want to communicate in multiplayer games.

📈 Pros:
• Great positional audio and imaging for competitive gaming.
• Open design provides a natural, spacious soundstage.
• Comfortable for long play sessions.
• Wired connection means zero latency audio.

📉 Cons:
• No built-in mic — requires a separate solution for voice chat.
• Open-back design leaks sound and won’t isolate noise in busy environments.
• Neutral tuning means less bass punch than some gamers might expect.

📍 Verdict:
If you’re a gamer who prioritizes accurate audio cues and competitive awareness over booming bass or flashy RGB headsets, the Sennheiser HD 560S is a fantastic upgrade — especially for PC titles and competitive shooters. Just pair them with a dedicated mic and you’ve essentially got a high-performance gaming audio setup.

reviews.author: John Doe
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on December 6, 2025
reviews.content:
I consider these the best overall open back headphones I've owned. In the past I've used the HD600 and HD6xx from Sennheiser and the Beyerdynamic DT880, and they all did certain things very well but the lack of bass was frustrating. These lower priced Sennheisers don't have quite the sparkly treble of the others (in particular the Beyers) but have very satisfying bass and lower midrange that the others lacked, and for me personally this makes up for what they don't do as well. As always with headphones YMMV based on your personal preferences, but I prefer these to more expensive models.

They're also lightweight and comfortable for long sessions. I find I notice the clamping feeling when I first put them on, but it fades almost immediately.

reviews.author: Steve Taylor
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on March 2, 2024
reviews.content:
Yes you can do better but for the price it would be very difficult. My Sundara's are better in every way but they are also twice as much. My Focal Elex are in a different class all together. (These are all open back headphones.) The 560s is open back but more of a semi-open like the DT880.

PROS:
Quality Sennheiser plastic build that will last a lifetime.
Very lightweight.
Pleasantly comfortable.
Dimpled headband foam for relieving skull pain.
Very ample earcup size.
Swivels well.
Fits every size melon.
Felt earpads pad well. (I picked up sheepskin leather pads because I prefer them for comfort, coolness, longevity and sound.)
Very neutral sound signature for those that want to hear the music like the engineers wanted it to sound.
Plenty of bass for those that listen to adult music.
Since they are open they sound nice and wide.
At 120 ohms you can drive them from your phone but a good DAP or amp will help. The IFI Zen DAC 2 makes them sound rich which would be good to round-out a desktop system.
If you are familiar with the Sennheiser Sound and like it, you'll love these.
Very inexpensive when on sale.

CONS:
The cable and adapter stink. I'm not even going to use it.
Felt earpads are warm so not for everyone.
No extras included.

I own and have owned many headphones. My first audiophile headphone was a Sennheiser production model back in the 80s. If it wasn't 600 ohms I'd still own it. For the price you can't beat the 560s. I think it has a more natural sound then the 6xx which is highly regarded.

Highly recommended

reviews.author: Corey Strohecker
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on December 28, 2025
reviews.content: Clear and very tunable you your listening preferences. Open back allows sound to come through from your environment which is the trade off of having a better soundstage and more directional accuracy and allowing your ears to breathe and not feel over pressured during listening. Very light and comfy earpads that may be too warm for some being velour ear pads, but they are very soft and the headband is comfy as well. Good price for premium sounds.

reviews.author: J. Richardson
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on March 26, 2025
reviews.content:
From the dawn of time, every headphones set ever made has faced the same challenge: how to faithfully reproduce bass so the listener can clearly hear the beat without overpowering the mids or vocals, which muddies the audio. Nearly every review I've read online complains about these headphones' lack of bass. Those reviews are wrong. There's plenty of bass in these *if you drive them with the right source*. Their impedance is 120 ohms, which is much higher than even the legendary Sony MDR-7506's 63 ohms. Thus, if your source has insufficient power (which probably includes most phones and many laptops) you won't hear much out of them. However, they sound just right on my desktop PC through my Creative Sound Blaster AE-7 PCIe sound card. They're the 1st headphones of any kind in over 20 years I haven't had to boost the bass on at the source. At the same time, vocals are crystal. In fact, I'd say these headphones have the best vocals and definitely the best combination of bass and vocals of any pair I've owned. They're also super light, which is great for long periods of listening. I've seen some reviews questioning durability, but if you're physically breaking headphones the problem is YOU, not the headphones. Build quality (fit and finish) are superb. The velour earpads are super comfortable, though I worry about them getting stinky and grimy over time from sweat and skin oil. I really think AirWeave would have been a better choice at this price point, but I've only ever seen that material used in aftermarket pads.

I really struggle to think of any drawbacks with these, but here are some: there's no carrying case for the headphones and nowhere to put the 1/4 inch adapter when it's not attached to the cable. Both are odd omissions on a ~$200 product in this category. Some people might not appreciate the plasticky feel, but again headphones are supposed to be on your head, not in your hand, and everything that comes in contact with your head is plush. As these are open back headphones, sound isolation isn't guaranteed. Others will be able to hear your music (though it's not as loud as you might think), and you might be able to hear them (cranked to the max, it's likely you won't).

I'd comfortably call these the best headphones at their price point and perhaps up to $300, as they easily trump my Audeze Maxwells. If you're on the fence, have an amp, receiver, or dedicated sound card, and don't mind their open back config, pull the trigger. You'll love them.

reviews.author: Paul
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on October 18, 2024
reviews.content: Listening system: Streaming 24 bit/192khz 70s rock via Tidal connect through the Wiim Pro Plus streamer via optical connection to a 1mii DS601 dac analog to Denon AVR-x4500h (originally intended for little dot mk2 tube amplifier with upgraded matched pair russian tubes bought from Canada). I am returning these 560s headphones for a pair of massdrop x sennheiser HD 6XX Headphones. The massdrop x HD 6XX are similar to the sennheiser HD 650 headphones but cost significantly less from drop.com as they are branded as massdrop rather than Sennheiser and are about several hundred dollars less. The Sennheiser 560s headphones are great but my intention is to pair these headphones with a tube amp. The 560s headphones aren’t the best for tube amps, when compared to the HD 6XX headphones as the 560s headphones are meant to be neutral and analytical sounding for critical music listening and I am aiming for warm and lush vinyl like analog sound, which the massdrop x HD 6XX excel at when paired with a pure tube amp like the little dot mkii. The 560s headphones have 38mm drivers and 120 ohms making it easy to drive with a laptop or dac. The Massdrop x sennheiser HD 6XX (similar to the sennheiser HD650) have a 300 ohm impedance meaning it requires more power to reach its full potential so it greatly benefits from a dedicated headphone amp like the little dot mkii tube amp and has the same 42mm drivers as the HD650. In conclusion, the Sennheiser HD 560s headphones are designed for critical listeners and audiophiles on a budget. The Massdrop X Sennheiser HD 6XX headphones are designed for audiophiles, critical listeners, and enthusiasts. The sound signature from the 560s headphones are more neatral, with an analytical sound with tight bass, balanced mids, and smooth treble. The HD 6XX sound signature is warmer, with more musical sound and fuller bass, with rich mids, and smooth treble. The 560s is designed for critical listening, mixing, and mastering due to its high accuracy. The massdrop X Sennheiser HD 6XX is more premium as it has the same drivers as the HD650, weight, and feel, yet cheaper due to the branding, shorter cable, and color, and is designed to be more engaging for casual listening and genres that benefit from a slightly colored sound, like 70s rock. If you prioritize accuracy and detail, the HD 560S may be the better option. If you prefer a warmer, more enjoyable sound signature, the HD 6XX would be a great choice. Either way these two headphones are both great, in the end my goal is to listen to a warm, vinyl like sound, and enjoy the benefits of my upgraded tube amp so I ultimately decided to go with the sennheiser massdrop x HD 6xx headphones. That said, I loved listening to music in rock mode using the 560s headphones while connected to the denon AVR-x4500h, I did not have a chance to listen to the 560s headphones with my tube amp as it is on preorder and did not arrive on time for me to listen to the difference. Caution, both headphones do get warm around your ears during long listening sessions. Hope this helps you decide on what you are looking for in headphones and what application they will be used for and which is best. Thank you! I gave these headphones 5 stars as they sound amazing!

reviews.author: Social science professor
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on June 27, 2025
reviews.content:
The Focal Bathys MG are an audiophile bluetooth headphone with an audiophile price. That price will be a deal-breaker for the mass market but may very well appeal to audiophiles willing to spend big money for exceptional, beautiful sounding headphones.

Out of the box, they sound great, and the sound quality improves with break-in. I never imagined bluetooth headphones could sound this great. You can also use the included USB-C cable to plug them directly into your smartphone, bypassing the smartphone's DAC and using the built-in Focal DAC; this takes the SQ up a few notches.

The package also includes a cord with a minijack that you can plug into your stereo for wired listening if you wish. Weirdly, the power must be on for this; so the Bathys MG is not a passive headphone even when used with the analog cable.

The Bathys MG have a brilliant soundstage that's wide and deep; instruments and voices sound clear and you can identify where each one is in space - true with orchestral music and even more so with smaller ensembles like jazz quartets, folk or rock bands. The bass is tight and punchy but not at all booming or out of proportion. The mids are lush and clear; acoustic pianos and guitars sound amazingly clear and beautiful. Highs are crisp. The sound has a warmth to it, I wouldn't say it's a bias, but closer to analog than any active headphones I've heard.

Big disclaimer: I did not test other headphones in the same price range; that was probably dumb considering how much I spent on these. It's possible that similarly priced wireless headphones by Dali, Mark Levinson, Meze Audio, Audio Technica and the like sound better; I'd urge you to visit your local hi-fi shop and listen to a few of the competing headphones in the same price range before spending your hard-earned money.

Like all Focal headphones, the Bathys MG are visually beautiful, almost a work of art.

Regarding comfort: They weigh a bit more than the mostly plastic Sony & Bose mass-market headphones, but not *much* heavier, and the headband distributes the weight nicely; I found them comfortable for hours-long listening sessions. My head is a bit bigger than average, but I didn’t experience any discomfort from clamping. My wife’s is average for a woman and she found the fit perfectly comfortable and secure.

The noise cancellation and transparency mode are good (grade = B+), but in this category only they are surpassed by the Sony XM series or Bose Quiet Comfort series of portable BT headphones; I’ve owned these in the recent past and would give them an A for noise cancellation and transparency mode.

The carry case is a very protective hard shell case. It is nice looking and functional. It has just enough room for the headphones and the two cables I mentioned above (analog mini-jack and USB).

Like all companies that sell wireless headphones, Focal makes an app for smartphones. I found it easy to use. It lets you adjust the EQ to your liking and customize some of the controls and options.

Now we get to the big question. Are these worth the money?

For me, sadly no. I love the way these sound. I was in heaven for about two weeks, before I decided to return them. I have a pretty good audiophile stereo system, which I will always choose over any headphones when listening at home. To listen when my wife is sleeping, I have passive/wired Hifi-man planar headphones that are pretty good with my stereo.

So my main use-case for wireless headphones is when traveling or at the office. In those scenarios, sound quality is less important and convenience is more important.

So I returned the Bathys MG and bought the new Sony XM6. The Sonys as you know are a mass market consumer-grade product. They cost 1/3 as much as the Bathys MG. IMHO, they're much better for traveling, they're lighter and more compact, and the noise cancellation is top-notch on a plane, train, or bus. The SQ is excellent for a consumer grade BT headphone, and for me that’s good enough on an airplane or train.

If you doubt that you'd get $1500 of value from the Bathys MG, you might consider the original Bathys, which list for $699 but can often be found for around $600 - including here on Amazon. It's also a bluetooth headphone with a USB-C cable and an internal DAC that blows away the DAC in your smartphone. The SQ approaches that of the much more expensive MG model and has many of the same SQ characteristics I described above.

The MG weren't right for me, but they sound ridiculous (I mean that in the best possible way). For audiophiles who do a lot of headphone listening and would value getting audiophile sound quality wirelessly from a smartphone or similar device, the Focal Bathys MG are worth your very strong consideration.

reviews.author: chchon
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on December 6, 2025
reviews.content:
I loved my Bathys OG so much that I had been itching to give the MG’s a try since they were introduced last spring. When the price dropped $300 with Amazon’s Black Friday promotion, I jumped.

Are these MG’s noticeably better than the OG? Yes, although the improvements are not dramatic, the MG's are more comfortable, the ANC is slightly better, and the sound is clearer, more detailed, richer, and more spacious. Also, the sound separation among instruments also is better. That is not to knock to OG, it just reflects that the MG’s are, indeed, a step up. I also appreciate the MG’s 3db volume increments versus the 5db on the OG, a difference that makes it easier to dial in just the right volume for any given selection. This latter factor is more important than you might think initially.

I also own a pair of Focal Hadenys, which are wired to my video system. Compared to both Bathys models, the open-backed Haydenys deliver an even richer sound, better timbre, a slightly larger soundstage and noticeably better bass, but they are less comfortable (overly mushy ear pads don’t complement the clamping force). For me, the MG’s portability and comfort make them my go-to headphones for most listening outside of movies. In fact, both the OG’s and MG’s seem to highlight vocals more so than the Hadenys, which stand out more so with purely instrumental music. Each of these realities underscores why I prefer all Focal options over my now-retired Sennheiser HD 700s and HD 560S2s, neither of which delivered the listening experience I prefer.

Are the MG’s improvements worth double or triple the price of the OG’s? I would say probably not unless the price difference does not affect your budget that much. But if you are comfortable spending $1,000+ for wireless headphones, the MG's should be a top choice.

reviews.author: the finer things✨🕶
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on August 2, 2025
reviews.content:
I want to try to keep this as concise as possible (although it is temping to write poetry about these)...

Out of the box, the sound quality is absolutely tremendous, exactly what I was hoping for. (How often do you buy a pricey pair of headphones and are ultimately a bit letdown? With these, I was only impressed -- and after the 'burn in' (when wired, I gave them about 24 hours of play before really settling into using them), they sounded even better: deeper, richer, a wider soundstage.

Pretty much every facet of these headphones is so outstanding that there is, after owning them for a week, nothing to really criticize ... if I were to be extremely picky, and I guess that's what we're here to do, I'd say the hardshell case they included is a little underwhelming in that it fits the headphones TOO snugly and feels like a bit of an afterthought. (They could have upgraded the case from the regular Bathys; it's the same one.) But does that matter? No. Because you're buying these for sound quality and frankly, I've never heard music so intricate and detailed through a Bluetooth device. The sound is even better, naturally, when used in DAC mode (I would recommend upgrading the USB C and headphone cables to something more premium if you really want these to soar -- there is a small but audible difference when you wear these wired between what's included and more premium cables).

My hat is off to Focal. Essentially combining the Clear MGs with the Bathys, they've managed to make something better than both of those models -- you'll be floored.

reviews.author: Thomas V.
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on March 6, 2026
reviews.content:
These sound fantastic. I’ve had the Focal Bathys for a couple years and was in the market to get another headset. I tried the B&W PX8 S2 as I’ve been looking forward to trying them but they didn’t meet my expectations; the bass outshines the FB but at the cost of tinny and muddy highs. Though I appreciate they taking my return after brief use.

I considered getting a second set of the FB AM due to the price but figured I’d try the FB MG. I’m glad I decided to go with the MG. These are expensive but totally worth it for the experience. They sound quality separates it from anything I’ve heard. The highs are crisp and the bass still hits. This set complements the genres I listen to. Pair (ba dun ts) that with the frequency and lengths of use and it’s a great value to me.

I plan to try out the DALI IO-12 in the not too distant future so who knows… those may surpass this set for my use. I’d be pleasantly surprised if the case.

reviews.author: Wes Mal
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on December 31, 2025
reviews.content: These headphones live up to the online hype by delivering excellent sound replication. Really comfortable fit over my ears as well. They would certainly be a better value at $300-$400 less. But, so far I have no regrets.

reviews.author: Khoa A. Dang
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on December 25, 2025
reviews.content: The Focal Bathys MG headphones are a big improvement over the formerly acclaimed wireless Blue Tooth pairs of cans from Sony, the WH-1000XM5, as it should be since the Focal costs about almost four times more than the Sony. The bass is well defined and tight, not too boomy like the Sony. The highs are more extended and airier and transparent. The vocals and mids are more articulate and stand out on their own not being obscure by the instrumental sound. Noise cancellation is very good. The Focal is as close to a pair of excellent wired headphones as one's could get. Just lacking the ultimate sweetness and involving emotion of the vocals from a pair of audiophile wired headphones like the Meze Audio Empyrean or the Denon AH-D9200, but these also cost about 20 to 25% more than the Focal and lack the convenience of movement of the Focal. If you value sound quality then go with a pair of audiophile wired headphones in the $1800 to $2500 range. But if you want convenience then the Focal is among the best of wireless headphones.

reviews.author: Jimmy
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on October 20, 2025
reviews.content: Excellence in listening, extremely comfortable beauty, top notch

reviews.author: Nelson
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on December 23, 2025
reviews.content: As expected these are crisp, detailed and bass is better than my ML °5909 or Dali IO-12s. Definitely in top 3 of all wireless headphones under 2k. Ive tested dozens.

reviews.author: SuzyQue
reviews.date: Reviewed in the United States on July 12, 2021
reviews.content:
The hardware in the Krakens is excellent, imo. My husband loves these phones (and I do, too!) My previous phones, a pair of Sony MDR-7506's, were so good we thought nothing could beat them, even though the Sony phones' drivers are 40 mms in size and these Razer Kraken drivers are 50mm--much depends on the amplification hardware in the ear pads and motherboard hardware as to which will drive the better sound--eg., 50 mm drivers are not automatically better than 40mm drivers, regardless of the hardware environments. A lot also depends on the preferences of the individuals asked to state their opinions, of course. Some people like bass so overwhelming it actually distorts whereas other people like it only enough to hear it plainly with no distortion, and others like it turned down or off, inexplicably, etc. (But you can't argue with individual opinions, right?...Opinions are like armpits--we all have at least two of them, eh? So, I advise listening to all sides and colors of opinions, especially about computer hardware! There's always something new to learn even we don't agree with the overall opinions other people have or their conclusions. Opinions should always be debated--never, ever censored, we firmly believe!)

Bear in mind, both sets of phones were tested via my husband's x570 Aorus Master motherboard's RealTek'HD Audio 3.5mm analog Hi-Definition front-panel jack--the best earphone connection jack in the system, btw. (Give me wired over wireless any day--I hate batteries and frequency interference, not to mention security concerns that do not exist with wired connections, etc.) The x570 Master also supports, in hardware, a 3-position headphone amp, and a hardware DAC, a SABRE 9118 DAC--the combination has to be heard to be properly appreciated, imo.

Connector cabling. The Kraken offers a superior wiring connection--Kraken uses an extremely thin and ultra-flexible braided connector cable, whereas the Sony offers a thick, plastic-covered connection cable in which most of the length is coiled, just like analog amp cables circa the 1960's were made. Indeed, the Sony cabling doesn't look like something made for a 3.5mm plug! It looks exactly like something made for a full size plug! It's opinion, sure, as everything always is in the end, but we much prefer the Kraken's cabling over that used in the Sony cans used as the baseline comparison for this review. The Kraken cabling is ultralight, ultrathin, cloth braided, and so ultra-flexible that there simply isn't any obstinate stiffness in the cable that can be perceived. We both like it very much because of that--it is the nearest thing to not having a cable at all, but without any of the negatives of wireless.
